transcription_cleaned:
Doctor: Good morning. How can I help you today?
Patient: Good morning, doctor. I was referred here from another hospital. I'm 27 weeks pregnant and I had a very scary episode a few weeks ago.
Doctor: I'm glad you came in. Can you walk me through what happened?
Patient: At 22 weeks, I started having vaginal bleeding. I went to the emergency room and they admitted me for bedrest. About two days later, I got terrible pelvic pain and contractions, and I passed a large mass vaginally.
Doctor: That sounds very distressing. Were there any fetal parts in the mass?
Patient: No, the doctors said there were no fetal contents. The baby was fine — they checked the heart tones and everything was okay.
Doctor: Do you know what the pathology showed on that mass?
Patient: Yes, they told me it was a very aggressive-looking tumor under the microscope, with spindle-shaped cells and giant cells. The pathologist said it could be an endometrial stromal sarcoma or an undifferentiated uterine sarcoma, but they weren't sure.
Doctor: I see. And what happened after that — did they do any procedure at the outside hospital?
Patient: Yes, they took me to the operating room and found a hole in the back wall of my vagina that was bleeding. They stitched it closed. My cervix was closed and the baby was still okay.
Doctor: Good. Can you tell me about your obstetric history?
Patient: This is my fourth pregnancy. I've had two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage.
Doctor: Any prior gynecologic problems — abnormal pap smears, fibroids, infertility?
Patient: No, nothing like that. My periods were always regular, no pain with sex, no unusual bleeding before this pregnancy.
Doctor: Any family history of cancer?
Patient: Yes, actually. Two of my family members had cervical cancer and two had endometrial cancer.
Doctor: That's important to know. We'll order an MRI today to get a better picture of what's happening. Has the fetus had any issues noted on ultrasound?
Patient: Yes, one of the baby's kidneys looks abnormal — they said it's dysplastic.
Doctor: Okay. And how are you feeling now at 27 weeks?
Patient: I feel okay. No more bleeding, no pelvic pain. I'm just worried about what this mass was.
Doctor: Understood. We're going to coordinate with our gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine teams to make the best plan for you and your baby.
Patient: Thank you, doctor. I just want to make sure we're both safe.
clinical_summary: A 31-year-old G4P0 woman presented at 27 weeks gestation with a history of vaginal bleeding and expulsion of a large mass at 22 weeks, initially suspected to be a sarcoma. Pathology was inconclusive, and she underwent a cesarean hysterectomy at 36 weeks, which showed benign pathology. Postpartum, a high-grade vaginal sarcoma was diagnosed and treated with surgery and brachytherapy, with the patient remaining disease-free at 58 months. The fetus had a right multi-cystic dysplastic kidney.

differential_diagnosis.rationale: The postpartum diagnosis of high-grade vaginal sarcoma, treated successfully with surgery and brachytherapy, aligns with the clinical history of vaginal bleeding and mass expulsion during pregnancy.
Initially suspected due to the presentation and inconclusive pathology, endometrial stromal sarcoma remains a differential given the uterine involvement and the initial clinical suspicion.
The initial inconclusive pathology suggested this possibility, and while later findings were benign, the aggressive nature of the postpartum sarcoma could align with this diagnosis.

final_report.subjective: The patient is a 31-year-old woman who is currently 27 weeks pregnant. She reports a significant episode of vaginal bleeding at 22 weeks gestation, during which she passed a large mass vaginally. She was admitted to the hospital for bedrest and subsequently experienced pelvic pain and contractions. The mass was examined and found to contain no fetal parts, and the baby's heart tones were normal. The patient has a history of two therapeutic abortions and one miscarriage. She has no prior gynecologic issues such as abnormal pap smears or fibroids. There is a family history of cervical and endometrial cancer. The patient is concerned about the nature of the mass and the health of her baby, who has been noted to have a dysplastic kidney on ultrasound.
final_report.objective: On examination, the patient is stable at 27 weeks gestation with no current vaginal bleeding or pelvic pain. A pelvic exam revealed no residual vaginal mass. An MRI of the pelvis, abdomen, and chest showed a Nabothian cyst in the cervix and a multiloculated cystic structure in the fetal kidney, consistent with a dysplastic kidney. There was no evidence of metastatic disease. Pathology from the expelled mass was inconclusive, showing a highly cellular tumor with spindle-shaped and multinucleated giant cells. Immunohistochemistry was positive for CD10, SMA, ER, PR, and EMA, suggesting a differential diagnosis of endometrial stromal sarcoma versus undifferentiated uterine sarcoma.
final_report.assessment: The patient presents with a complex case of vaginal bleeding and mass expulsion during pregnancy, initially suspected to be a sarcoma. The pathology was inconclusive, but the clinical presentation and family history raise suspicion for a high-grade vaginal sarcoma. The fetus has a right multi-cystic dysplastic kidney, which is a separate concern. The patient's condition requires careful monitoring and coordination with gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine specialists to ensure the safety of both the mother and the fetus.
final_report.plan: The plan includes ordering an MRI to further evaluate the pelvic mass and coordinating care with gynecologic oncology and maternal-fetal medicine teams. The patient will be monitored closely for any changes in symptoms. A cesarean hysterectomy is planned at 36 weeks to manage the suspected sarcoma and ensure safe delivery of the baby. Betamethasone will be administered to promote fetal lung maturity. Postpartum, the patient will undergo further evaluation and treatment for the sarcoma, including possible surgery and brachytherapy, depending on the final pathology results. The fetal kidney anomaly will be managed with pediatric nephrology consultation after birth.
